metaDescription: Section 80C deductions guide for India with monthly allocation frameworks and scenario-based recommendations.

title: Capital Gains Tax in India: LTCG vs STCG for equity investing
metaDescription: Understand holding-period rules, tax bands, and sale-order planning before rebalancing.

title: The holding period question most investors get wrong
metaDescription: Capital gains planning is part of the broader tax picture — including regime choice, 80C optimization, and NPS.

title: The moratorium is not free — interest still accrues
metaDescription: Education loan interest is deductible for 8 years with no upper limit. Keep annual interest payment certificates and claim this deduction even on the new tax regime (for self/family education loans this benefit was retained).

title: The question everyone gets wrong: "How many months?"
metaDescription: A liquid savings account, a high-yield savings account with quick transfer, or a liquid mutual fund (T+1) are the right places. FD is acceptable if premature closure is cost-free or low-cost.

title: Both track the same index. That does not make them the same.
metaDescription: ELSS mutual funds (not ETFs) are the only equity route with Section 80C tax benefit. If tax-saving is a goal, ELSS mutual funds are the right vehicle — there is no ELSS ETF.

title: The problem with one large FD
metaDescription: Once your liquid emergency buffer and FD ladder are in place, surplus savings for 5+ year goals should move into equity SIP for better long-run returns.

title: GST adds 18% to many financial service costs — and most people do not notice
metaDescription: The 80D deduction applies to the premium including GST. Understand current limits and whether your regime allows the deduction.

title: The 7% savings rate that costs you money
metaDescription: FD laddering often gives better certain yield than a savings account for stable balances you can lock away temporarily.

title: The rate you see on the brochure is not the rate you will pay
metaDescription: Current RLLR-linked home loan rates from major lenders — along with spread, reset frequency, and effective rate context.

title: NPS is tax-efficient. It is also illiquid until 60.
metaDescription: Once NPS 80CCD(1B) is decided, build your ₹1.5L 80C allocation using PPF, ELSS, or EPF depending on timeline and risk appetite.

title: Most people choose a tax regime in February. Most get it wrong.
metaDescription: Automate 80C contributions by April. Split your ₹1.5L limit across EPF (auto), PPF (monthly), and ELSS (monthly SIP). Do not leave it for February.

title: Credit card debt in India costs more than most people realize
metaDescription: Once credit card and personal loan debt is under control, redirect surplus toward emergency buffer first, then SIP.

title: PPF vs ELSS: India Tax-Saving Decision Guide for 2026
metaDescription: Compare PPF and ELSS using lock-in reality, family cashflow needs, and practical ₹ allocation examples for Section 80C planning.

title: The most expensive SIP mistake is starting too large
metaDescription: Separate your tax-saving 80C contribution from your wealth SIP. Then check whether ELSS, PPF, or a split makes sense for your timeline and risk tolerance.

title: The question most guides answer wrong
metaDescription: If your SIP horizon is 10+ years, learn how to set up automation, step up contributions annually, and stay invested through volatility without panic-exiting.

metaDescription: Use India EMI and SIP calculators as decision engines: test assumptions, read outcomes, and move to the right comparison or guide next.
